meta.title: Southeast Asia Displacement Governance Mapping
meta.description: Framework-derived qualitative governance database: 8 countries, 7 governance areas, 3 fault lines, and chapter-linked case examples.
meta.method: Qualitative governance assessment derived from the accompanying chapter, policy briefs, secondary sources, and practitioner analysis. Not a ranking, scorecard, or primary data collection.

dimensions.name: Mobility governance
dimensions.desc: How migration, refugee, asylum, statelessness, and cross-border movement are recognised and governed.

dimensions.name: Disaster & internal displacement
dimensions.desc: How disaster, conflict, and internal displacement are governed across emergency and recovery phases.

dimensions.name: Public systems integration
dimensions.desc: How health, education, registration, labour, and social protection systems include or exclude displaced populations.

dimensions.name: Institutional responsibility
dimensions.desc: How mandates and coordination are allocated across ministries, local authorities, and external actors.

dimensions.name: Fiscal architecture
dimensions.desc: How displacement-related obligations are financed, transferred, bounded, or left outside ordinary budgets.

dimensions.name: Data & administrative infrastructure
dimensions.desc: How registries, identity systems, databases, and eligibility architecture follow — or fail to follow — people.

dimensions.name: Infrastructure & development finance
dimensions.desc: How safeguards, project finance, land acquisition, and development corridors shape displacement risk and absorption.

countries.name: Thailand
countries.role: Strongest absorption case: Health Insurance Card Scheme; pink cards/statelessness; Karen return reset; 2025-26 work-permit and biometric ID formalisation for camp-based Myanmar Displaced Persons.
countries.summary: Thailand combines restrictive refugee law with comparatively strong sectoral absorption in health, now extending to a phased work-permit and civil-registry formalisation for long-staying camp residents from Myanmar. Its governance pattern shows how inclusion can advance through administrative, public-health, and labour-market incentives while remaining categorically separate from citizenship or asylum status -- and how that incentive-led inclusion can deepen (registry integration, banking and service access) without yet becoming a general legal pathway.
cases: 2025-26 refugee work-permit and biometric ID formalisation

mobility.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): the Aug 2025 cabinet resolution and Oct 2025 implementation granted work permits to long-staying Myanmar refugees in the nine border camps; the Royal Thai Government began issuing biometric, non-Thai ID cards in Jun 2026 that formally recognise camp residents as "Myanmar Displaced Persons" and register them in the national civil registry. This is the most significant formalisation of camp-based refugees' status since the camps were established in the 1980s, moving a defined cohort from administrative tolerance toward documented status with civil-registry linkage. The categorical fault line narrows for this cohort but does not close: status remains non-citizen, phased by age and employment, and distinct from asylum recognition or the National Screening Mechanism. Non-refoulement practice remains inconsistent, as the Feb 2025 deportation of long-detained Uyghur asylum seekers illustrates.
mechanisms: Aug 2025 cabinet resolution on refugee work rights
Jun 2026 biometric non-Thai ID card / civil registry integration
mobility.sources: UNHCR Thailand, "Government-issued ID cards mark new era of refugee inclusion and self-reliance in Thailand," press release, 17 Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/th/en/government-issued-id-cards-mark-new-era-of-refugee-inclusion%20and%20self-reliance-in-thailand | UNHCR, "UNHCR welcomes Royal Thai Government resolution providing long-term refugees the right to work," press release, 26 Aug 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/news/press-releases/unhcr-welcomes-royal-thai-government-resolution-providing-long-term-refugees | Human Rights Watch, "Thailand Allows Myanmar Refugees in Camps to Work Legally," 27 Aug 2025, https://www.hrw.org/news/2025/08/27/thailand-allows-myanmar-refugees-in-camps-to-work-legally | The Diplomat, "Thai Government Grants Refugees From Myanmar the Right to Work," 27 Aug 2025, https://thediplomat.com/2025/08/thai-government-grants-refugees-from-myanmar-the-right-to-work/ | UNHCR, "UNHCR condemns forced returns from Thailand," press release, 27 Feb 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/us/news/press-releases/unhcr-condemns-forced-returns-thailand

disaster_idp.characterization: Disaster governance is comparatively institutionalized through the Ministry of Interior, but internal displacement is not governed through a distinct longitudinal framework. Stateless and border populations are managed through registration instruments that can restrict movement and reset access across provinces.

public_systems.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): civil-registry integration accompanying the new ID cards is designed to enable access to banking, telecommunications, and healthcare for the camp-based cohort, alongside continued HICS coverage. This adds a second, registry-linked absorption channel alongside the existing premium-based HICS model, though it remains bounded to a defined and phased population rather than a general extension of public-system access to all non-Thai displaced persons.
mechanisms: Health Insurance Card Scheme for Non-Thai Persons
Jun 2026 biometric non-Thai ID / civil registry (banking, telecom, healthcare access)
public_systems.sources: UNHCR Thailand, "Government-issued ID cards mark new era of refugee inclusion and self-reliance in Thailand," press release, 17 Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/th/en/government-issued-id-cards-mark-new-era-of-refugee-inclusion%20and%20self-reliance-in-thailand | UNHCR, "UNHCR welcomes Royal Thai Government resolution providing long-term refugees the right to work," press release, 26 Aug 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/news/press-releases/unhcr-welcomes-royal-thai-government-resolution-providing-long-term-refugees | Human Rights Watch, "Thailand Allows Myanmar Refugees in Camps to Work Legally," 27 Aug 2025, https://www.hrw.org/news/2025/08/27/thailand-allows-myanmar-refugees-in-camps-to-work-legally | The Diplomat, "Thai Government Grants Refugees From Myanmar the Right to Work," 27 Aug 2025, https://thediplomat.com/2025/08/thai-government-grants-refugees-from-myanmar-the-right-to-work/

institutional.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): the cabinet-level work-rights resolution (2025) and the civil-registry ID rollout (2026) were both executive/administrative actions rather than legislative reform, consistent with the existing pattern of sectoral and cabinet-discretionary coordination. The Ministry of Labour (work permits) and Ministry of Interior (civil registration) are now both load-bearing for this cohort's status, alongside the pre-existing screening and health-ministry channels -- broadening, not replacing, the sectoral coordination pattern. No single longitudinal mandate has been created.
institutional.sources: UNHCR Thailand, "Government-issued ID cards mark new era of refugee inclusion and self-reliance in Thailand," press release, 17 Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/th/en/government-issued-id-cards-mark-new-era-of-refugee-inclusion%20and%20self-reliance-in-thailand | UNHCR, "UNHCR welcomes Royal Thai Government resolution providing long-term refugees the right to work," press release, 26 Aug 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/news/press-releases/unhcr-welcomes-royal-thai-government-resolution-providing-long-term-refugees | Human Rights Watch, "Thailand Allows Myanmar Refugees in Camps to Work Legally," 27 Aug 2025, https://www.hrw.org/news/2025/08/27/thailand-allows-myanmar-refugees-in-camps-to-work-legally | The Diplomat, "Thai Government Grants Refugees From Myanmar the Right to Work," 27 Aug 2025, https://thediplomat.com/2025/08/thai-government-grants-refugees-from-myanmar-the-right-to-work/

fiscal.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): the work-permit model preserves bounded fiscal exposure by tying camp residents' formal status to employment and a defined population (around 80,000 in nine camps, an estimated 42,000 of working age) rather than creating an open-ended entitlement; revenue effects run through payroll and consumption rather than new transfer obligations. This extends, rather than departs from, the bounded-inclusion logic already established through HICS premium/cost-recovery financing.
mechanisms: Cost recovery through public health facilities
fiscal.sources: UNHCR Thailand, "Government-issued ID cards mark new era of refugee inclusion and self-reliance in Thailand," press release, 17 Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/th/en/government-issued-id-cards-mark-new-era-of-refugee-inclusion%20and%20self-reliance-in-thailand | UNHCR, "UNHCR welcomes Royal Thai Government resolution providing long-term refugees the right to work," press release, 26 Aug 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/news/press-releases/unhcr-welcomes-royal-thai-government-resolution-providing-long-term-refugees | Human Rights Watch, "Thailand Allows Myanmar Refugees in Camps to Work Legally," 27 Aug 2025, https://www.hrw.org/news/2025/08/27/thailand-allows-myanmar-refugees-in-camps-to-work-legally | The Diplomat, "Thai Government Grants Refugees From Myanmar the Right to Work," 27 Aug 2025, https://thediplomat.com/2025/08/thai-government-grants-refugees-from-myanmar-the-right-to-work/

data.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): the new ID-card system adds biometric registration, QR-code verification, and civil-registry integration for camp-based Myanmar Displaced Persons -- the first instance of a camp population being folded into the national civil registry rather than managed solely through parallel camp/UNHCR documentation. This is a portability-relevant development: registry inclusion is the precondition for the public-system access (banking, telecom, health) the cards are intended to unlock. It does not yet resolve fragmentation across disaster, health, welfare, and migration records more broadly, and rollout is phased by age and employment status.
mechanisms: Jun 2026 biometric non-Thai ID card with QR verification, integrated into national civil registry
data.sources: UNHCR Thailand, "Government-issued ID cards mark new era of refugee inclusion and self-reliance in Thailand," press release, 17 Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/th/en/government-issued-id-cards-mark-new-era-of-refugee-inclusion%20and%20self-reliance-in-thailand | UNHCR, "UNHCR welcomes Royal Thai Government resolution providing long-term refugees the right to work," press release, 26 Aug 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/news/press-releases/unhcr-welcomes-royal-thai-government-resolution-providing-long-term-refugees | Human Rights Watch, "Thailand Allows Myanmar Refugees in Camps to Work Legally," 27 Aug 2025, https://www.hrw.org/news/2025/08/27/thailand-allows-myanmar-refugees-in-camps-to-work-legally | The Diplomat, "Thai Government Grants Refugees From Myanmar the Right to Work," 27 Aug 2025, https://thediplomat.com/2025/08/thai-government-grants-refugees-from-myanmar-the-right-to-work/

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure-induced displacement is less central than in Mekong dam states, but Thailand is affected by cross-border downstream impacts and return dynamics along the Myanmar border. Development finance is not yet systematically connected to displacement absorption.

countries.sources: UNHCR Thailand, "Government-issued ID cards mark new era of refugee inclusion and self-reliance in Thailand," press release, 17 Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/th/en/government-issued-id-cards-mark-new-era-of-refugee-inclusion%20and%20self-reliance-in-thailand | UNHCR, "UNHCR welcomes Royal Thai Government resolution providing long-term refugees the right to work," press release, 26 Aug 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/news/press-releases/unhcr-welcomes-royal-thai-government-resolution-providing-long-term-refugees | Human Rights Watch, "Thailand Allows Myanmar Refugees in Camps to Work Legally," 27 Aug 2025, https://www.hrw.org/news/2025/08/27/thailand-allows-myanmar-refugees-in-camps-to-work-legally | The Diplomat, "Thai Government Grants Refugees From Myanmar the Right to Work," 27 Aug 2025, https://thediplomat.com/2025/08/thai-government-grants-refugees-from-myanmar-the-right-to-work/

countries.name: Malaysia
countries.role: Categorical rigidity case: labour migration model extended to refugees; absorption ceiling shaped by citizenship and bumiputera politics; since Jan 2026, a state-run registration system (DPP) has displaced UNHCR as the primary registering authority without conferring legal status.
countries.summary: Malaysia is the clearest categorical rigidity case. Refugees are managed through immigration law rather than protection architecture, and even limited functional access preserves a sharp separation from legal or fiscal incorporation. The 2026 shift to government-controlled registration (DPP) changes who holds the documentation function -- from UNHCR to the state -- without changing the underlying categorical boundary: registration is not status, and mass enforcement continues alongside it.
cases: Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian (DPP), 2026 state-led registration replacing UNHCR registration role

mobility.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): in Jan 2026 the government began rolling out Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian (DPP), a government-run registration system developed by MIMOS Berhad that is intended to replace UNHCR's registration role outright. The Home Minister has stated DPP will be the sole recognized refugee documentation, with UNHCR's function narrowed to resettlement referrals only. This is a different mechanism from non-recognition: rather than leaving refugees outside any status system, the state is inserting itself as the sole registering authority while still declining to ratify the 1951 Convention or legislate refugee status. DPP cards do not confer legal status or a right to remain; they are framed as bridging documentation pending resettlement, in a context where resettlement departures fell sharply (1,970 in 2025, down from 8,627 in 2024) after US resettlement cuts. Mass enforcement has intensified in parallel (approx. 92,000 irregular-migrant arrests in 2025 vs 12,000 in 2021), including arrests of UNHCR cardholders, so the categorical fault line persists even as its administrative form changes.
mechanisms: No legal framework for refugee status
MKN Directive No. 23 (unpublished, 2023 revision nominally granting work rights)
Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian (DPP), Jan 2026 government-led registration system
mobility.sources: Human Rights Watch, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration System Raises Concerns," 4 May 2026, https://www.hrw.org/news/2026/05/04/malaysia-new-refugee-registration-system-raises-concerns | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: Rohingya Refugees Held Indefinitely in Inhumane and Degrading Conditions," 12 Mar 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-03-12/ | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration Scheme Must Protect Rights," 1 Jun 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-06-01/ | UNHCR Malaysia, "The UN Refugee Agency," accessed Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/my/ (confirms DPP launched Jan 2026 as government-led registration system) | New Straits Times, "Refugee policy proposal to be presented to cabinet by mid-June," 19 May 2026, https://www.nst.com.my/amp/news/nation/2026/05/1443346/refugee-policy-proposal-be-presented-cabinet-mid-june | ACAPS, "Malaysia," country page, accessed Jun 2026, https://www.acaps.org/en/countries/malaysia (over 210,000 registered refugees/asylum seekers as of Oct 2025, ~90% from Myanmar incl. 120,000+ Rohingya)

disaster_idp.characterization: Disaster management is organized around national disaster response but does not systematically include non-citizen or undocumented populations. Internal displacement is not a significant recognized policy category.

public_systems.characterization: Social protection programmes are overwhelmingly citizen or lawful-worker based. Refugees can access some health services at foreign-patient rates but are excluded from ordinary assistance, social insurance, and formal labour access.

institutional.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): the Home Affairs Ministry, via the national applied-research body MIMOS Berhad, has taken direct operational control of refugee registration through DPP, a function previously performed by UNHCR. This deepens immigration-led governance rather than diversifying it: a security- and documentation-control rationale (curbing fake documentation, responding to a National Security Council directive) now sits alongside the pre-existing immigration-enforcement mandate, with no separate protection-mandated institution holding parallel authority. SUHAKAM, Malaysia's human rights commission, has publicly criticized the secrecy of the underlying NSC Directive No. 23 as insufficient and untimely.
institutional.sources: Human Rights Watch, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration System Raises Concerns," 4 May 2026, https://www.hrw.org/news/2026/05/04/malaysia-new-refugee-registration-system-raises-concerns | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: Rohingya Refugees Held Indefinitely in Inhumane and Degrading Conditions," 12 Mar 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-03-12/ | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration Scheme Must Protect Rights," 1 Jun 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-06-01/ | UNHCR Malaysia, "The UN Refugee Agency," accessed Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/my/ (confirms DPP launched Jan 2026 as government-led registration system) | New Straits Times, "Refugee policy proposal to be presented to cabinet by mid-June," 19 May 2026, https://www.nst.com.my/amp/news/nation/2026/05/1443346/refugee-policy-proposal-be-presented-cabinet-mid-june | ACAPS, "Malaysia," country page, accessed Jun 2026, https://www.acaps.org/en/countries/malaysia (over 210,000 registered refugees/asylum seekers as of Oct 2025, ~90% from Myanmar incl. 120,000+ Rohingya)

fiscal.characterization: Fiscal architecture reinforces exclusion by reserving social assistance and insurance systems for citizens or documented workers. Refugee inclusion is framed as potential fiscal and membership exposure rather than resilience investment.

data.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): DPP collects biometrics into a government-controlled database, replacing the UNHCR-issued refugee card as the operative identification instrument for those registered. This is the first instance of Malaysia building its own state-held refugee identity system rather than relying on (and periodically disregarding) UNHCR documentation -- a shift from 'parallel identification without state effect' toward state-controlled identification without state-conferred status. No published data-protection safeguards, retention limits, or appeal mechanism have been identified for the system as of mid-2026; HRW wrote the Home Affairs Ministry on 21 Apr 2026 requesting clarification and did not receive a reply.
mechanisms: UNHCR refugee cards (pre-2026, now non-recognized by authorities for status purposes)
Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian (DPP) biometric database, operated via MIMOS Berhad
Refugee Registration Committees (status determination, Jan 2026 onward)
data.sources: Human Rights Watch, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration System Raises Concerns," 4 May 2026, https://www.hrw.org/news/2026/05/04/malaysia-new-refugee-registration-system-raises-concerns | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: Rohingya Refugees Held Indefinitely in Inhumane and Degrading Conditions," 12 Mar 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-03-12/ | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration Scheme Must Protect Rights," 1 Jun 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-06-01/ | UNHCR Malaysia, "The UN Refugee Agency," accessed Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/my/ (confirms DPP launched Jan 2026 as government-led registration system) | New Straits Times, "Refugee policy proposal to be presented to cabinet by mid-June," 19 May 2026, https://www.nst.com.my/amp/news/nation/2026/05/1443346/refugee-policy-proposal-be-presented-cabinet-mid-june | ACAPS, "Malaysia," country page, accessed Jun 2026, https://www.acaps.org/en/countries/malaysia (over 210,000 registered refugees/asylum seekers as of Oct 2025, ~90% from Myanmar incl. 120,000+ Rohingya)

infra_finance.characterization: Development-induced displacement is not the central mapped governance problem, though Orang Asli and land-related displacement raise protection questions outside refugee and migration systems.

countries.sources: Human Rights Watch, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration System Raises Concerns," 4 May 2026, https://www.hrw.org/news/2026/05/04/malaysia-new-refugee-registration-system-raises-concerns | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: Rohingya Refugees Held Indefinitely in Inhumane and Degrading Conditions," 12 Mar 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-03-12/ | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration Scheme Must Protect Rights," 1 Jun 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-06-01/ | UNHCR Malaysia, "The UN Refugee Agency," accessed Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/my/ (confirms DPP launched Jan 2026 as government-led registration system) | New Straits Times, "Refugee policy proposal to be presented to cabinet by mid-June," 19 May 2026, https://www.nst.com.my/amp/news/nation/2026/05/1443346/refugee-policy-proposal-be-presented-cabinet-mid-june

countries.name: Indonesia
countries.role: Palu, e-KTP/SIKS-NG, Presidential Regulation 125/2016, and fiscal-jurisdictional reset.
countries.summary: Indonesia has some of the region’s strongest formal architecture and data systems, but fiscal decentralisation and territorial anchoring limit continuity when people move across districts or emergency phases.

mobility.characterization: Indonesia is primarily a labour-sending state but has a formal refugee-handling instrument. Presidential Regulation 125/2016 assigns state responsibilities but does not create integration pathways or resolve local fiscal implementation gaps.

disaster_idp.characterization: BNPB provides strong emergency coordination, but support is time-bound. When emergency status expires, responsibility shifts to local government budgets, creating gaps for households whose displacement continues.

public_systems.characterization: Indonesia has significant social protection and health-system capacity through PKH, SIKS-NG, and BPJS Kesehatan, including adaptive enrolment in crises. The problem is not system absence but portability and eligibility continuity.

institutional.characterization: Formal whole-of-government design exists for refugees and disasters, but implementation is fragmented through decentralised fiscal and administrative authority across hundreds of districts.

fiscal.characterization: The fiscal constraint is jurisdictional. Emergency budgets can activate rapidly, but longer-term responsibility must be absorbed by local governments whose budget ceilings do not automatically follow displaced populations.

data.characterization: The e-KTP and SIKS-NG architecture is highly developed but territorially anchored. Disaster databases and social protection registries do not automatically interoperate, so displaced households can be reclassified or excluded at re-enumeration.

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure and development displacement are present but less central than disaster and data-system cases in the current chapter. The same absorption constraints would apply where project-affected populations require transfer into national systems.

countries.name: Philippines
countries.role: Marawi, 4Ps/Listahanan, post-Haiyan reconciliation, and OFW/IDP contrast.
countries.summary: The Philippines has advanced disaster, social protection, and overseas-worker institutions. Its core problem is not activation capacity but longitudinal continuity across local government, programme cycles, and recovery phases. The 2025 Anticipatory Action Law institutionalizes pre-disaster planning and financing -- a direct legislative response to this continuity gap -- though whether it resolves post-disaster sustained financing or only moves activation earlier in the cycle is not yet clear from public documentation.

mobility.characterization: Outward labour migration is governed through robust longitudinal institutions, while inward migration and domestic displacement receive far less portable protection. This contrast exposes the architecture of membership and portability.
mobility.pattern: External portability stronger than domestic portability

disaster_idp.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): the 2025 Anticipatory Action Law institutionalized pre-disaster planning and financing, a direct legislative response to the temporal drop-off this cell identifies (disaster response strong at activation, weaker on sustained financing into recovery). Whether the law closes the temporal gap or only moves it earlier in the disaster cycle (pre-positioning finance but still lacking sustained post-disaster transfer mechanisms) is not yet clear from public documentation and is worth tracking as implementation data emerges. Marawi compensation and temporary-shelter problems persist eight years on (approx. 8,200 people still displaced as of Mar 2025, paying rent on privately-owned land where shelters were built), confirming the existing characterization of activation without durable absorption rather than displacing it.
mechanisms: PDRRMA (Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act)
Anticipatory Action Law (2025), pre-disaster planning and financing
disaster_idp.sources: UN Philippines, Joint Work Plan 2026 Summary, 15 Apr 2026, https://philippines.un.org/sites/default/files/2026-04/UN-PHL-JWP%282026%29_Summary_Doc_20260415.pdf (confirms passage of the Anticipatory Action Law institutionalizing pre-disaster planning and financing; 23 tropical cyclones in 2025) | Australian Institute of International Affairs, "Philippines: Eight Years After the Conflict, Humanitarian Problems Remain in Marawi," 17 Jul 2025, https://www.internationalaffairs.org.au/australianoutlook/philippines-eight-years-after-the-conflict-humanitarian-problems-remain-in-marawi/ (approx. 8,200 people still in temporary shelters as of Mar 2025, paying rent on privately-owned land)

public_systems.characterization: 4Ps, Listahanan, PhilHealth, DepEd, and DSWD offer strong institutional capacity, but access is interrupted when displaced households cross municipal boundaries or fall between enumeration cycles.
public_systems.pattern: Partial fiscal absorption with portability gaps

institutional.characterization: Whole-of-government disaster coordination is formalized, yet recovery responsibility fragments across housing, education, health, welfare, and local government mandates. No single institution carries longitudinal IDP responsibility.

fiscal.characterization: The Philippines demonstrates both fiscal integration and its limits. 4Ps is legislated and budgeted, but devolved fiscal architecture creates portability gaps when displaced populations move across municipalities.

data.characterization: Listahanan provides a strong registry backbone and post-Haiyan data-matching precedent, but enumeration cycles and cross-municipal moves can interrupt benefits for displaced households.

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure finance is less central than disaster and conflict displacement in the chapter’s Philippines analysis, though reconstruction and housing after Marawi show how projectized recovery can fragment responsibility.

countries.sources: UN Philippines, Joint Work Plan 2026 Summary, 15 Apr 2026, https://philippines.un.org/sites/default/files/2026-04/UN-PHL-JWP%282026%29_Summary_Doc_20260415.pdf

countries.name: Myanmar
countries.role: Limit case -- institutional reversal post-2021, compounded by the Mar 2025 earthquake. Institutional reversal, Kachin/Shan protracted camps, social protection collapse, Rohingya statelessness, and now overlapping disaster and conflict displacement amid contracting humanitarian financing.
countries.summary: Myanmar is the framework's outer boundary. Where fiscal predictability, mandate stability, and sovereign continuity collapse, sustainable inclusion cannot be presumed; the mapping identifies what reconstruction would need to restore. The Mar 2025 earthquake compounded this collapse by adding disaster displacement on top of conflict displacement, projected to push internal displacement to approximately 4 million in 2026, at precisely the moment international humanitarian financing for Myanmar is itself contracting (only 26% of the 2025 appeal funded). Myanmar is therefore a limit case for both domestic fiscal collapse and the proposition that humanitarian financing can substitute for it indefinitely.
cases: Mar 2025 earthquake and 2026 humanitarian financing contraction

mobility.characterization: Migration and displacement are shaped by state-produced statelessness, conflict, labour emigration, and parallel post-coup governance. The 1982 Citizenship Law remains the core categorical exclusion mechanism for Rohingya.

disaster_idp.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): the Mar 2025 magnitude-7.7 earthquake compounded conflict-driven displacement, damaging tens of thousands of houses and nearly 70 health facilities and disrupting agriculture across central Myanmar. OCHA's 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan projects internal displacement rising from approximately 3.6 million to 4 million during 2026, and -- reflecting funding contraction rather than reduced need -- narrowed its operational scope to life-saving and protection activities in conflict- and earthquake-affected townships (about two-thirds of the country) rather than a nationwide caseload. This reinforces rather than changes the existing 'institutional reversal' characterization, but the earthquake and the 2026 scope narrowing are significant, undocumented additions: disaster and conflict displacement are now compounding in the same response plan, and humanitarian financing itself is contracting (only 26% of the 2025 appeal was funded) at the same time displacement needs are rising.
mechanisms: 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan (scope narrowed to conflict + earthquake townships)
disaster_idp.sources: OCHA, "Myanmar Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan 2026," 10 Dec 2025, https://www.unocha.org/publications/report/myanmar/myanmar-humanitarian-needs-and-response-plan-2026 | OCHA, "Myanmar Humanitarian Update no.51," 9 Mar 2026, https://www.unocha.org/publications/report/myanmar/myanmar-humanitarian-update-no51-9-march-2026 | World Food Programme, "WFP warns that Myanmar faces rising displacement and unacceptable hunger levels in 2026," https://www.wfp.org/news/wfp-warns-myanmar-faces-rising-displacement-and-unacceptable-hunger-levels-2026 | UN News, "'Almost invisible crisis': UN urges world not to look away from Myanmar," 17 Dec 2025, https://news.un.org/en/story/2025/12/1166618

public_systems.characterization: Pre-coup social protection expansion through MCCT, social pensions, and disability allowances collapsed after 2021. Health, education, and registration systems have fragmented into state, resistance, and humanitarian channels.

institutional.characterization: Whole-of-government coordination is structurally impossible under competing authorities and conflict. The mapping therefore reads Myanmar less as an absorption case than as a reconstruction requirements case.

fiscal.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): beyond the domestic fiscal collapse this cell already describes, the 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan's narrowed scope is itself a fiscal-architecture data point: international humanitarian financing for Myanmar is contracting (only 26% of the 2025 appeal was met) at precisely the moment the Mar 2025 earthquake has compounded conflict-driven need. Myanmar functions here as a limit case not only for domestic fiscal embedding but for the proposition -- central to the chapter's broader argument -- that humanitarian financing cannot substitute indefinitely for sovereign fiscal capacity; where both have collapsed simultaneously, no financing architecture currently absorbs the gap.
fiscal.sources: OCHA, "Myanmar Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan 2026," 10 Dec 2025, https://www.unocha.org/publications/report/myanmar/myanmar-humanitarian-needs-and-response-plan-2026 | OCHA, "Myanmar Humanitarian Update no.51," 9 Mar 2026, https://www.unocha.org/publications/report/myanmar/myanmar-humanitarian-update-no51-9-march-2026 | World Food Programme, "WFP warns that Myanmar faces rising displacement and unacceptable hunger levels in 2026," https://www.wfp.org/news/wfp-warns-myanmar-faces-rising-displacement-and-unacceptable-hunger-levels-2026 | UN News, "'Almost invisible crisis': UN urges world not to look away from Myanmar," 17 Dec 2025, https://news.un.org/en/story/2025/12/1166618

data.characterization: IDP data are dispersed across humanitarian registration systems, ethnic governance structures, and fragmented state records. No institutionalized transfer mechanism exists into stable national systems.

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure-related displacement exists, but post-coup conflict and institutional collapse dominate the governance landscape. Development finance entry points are constrained by legitimacy, sanctions, and access.
infra_finance.pattern: Reform entry point constrained by legitimacy crisis

countries.sources: OCHA, "Myanmar Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan 2026," 10 Dec 2025, https://www.unocha.org/publications/report/myanmar/myanmar-humanitarian-needs-and-response-plan-2026 | World Food Programme, "WFP warns that Myanmar faces rising displacement and unacceptable hunger levels in 2026," https://www.wfp.org/news/wfp-warns-myanmar-faces-rising-displacement-and-unacceptable-hunger-levels-2026

countries.name: Vietnam
countries.role: Hộ khẩu as non-interoperability by sovereign choice; planned relocation and infrastructure displacement.
countries.summary: Vietnam shows high state capacity directed toward territorial management rather than protection portability. Fragmentation is often deliberate: systems are designed to make populations legible to the state, not to track displacement vulnerability across boundaries.

mobility.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): the paper hokhau household registration book formally ceased validity on 1 Jan 2023, replaced by an ID-number-linked national population database under the 2020 Law on Residence. Ministry of Public Security officials have stated explicitly that this is a shift in administrative form -- paper to digital -- rather than an end to residence-tied management of access and mobility. The territorial legibility this cell describes therefore persists through the new instrument rather than being resolved by it; references to 'the household registration book' as a live mechanism should be updated to the post-2023 digital system, while the underlying characterization (state-directed legibility over portability) appears to hold.
mechanisms: ID-number-linked national population database (post-2023, replacing paper hokhau books)
Planned relocation and infrastructure displacement mechanisms (unchanged)
mobility.sources: Vietnam Law on Residence 2020 (effective 1 Jul 2021); household registration books ceased validity 1 Jan 2023, replaced by ID-number-linked national population database. VietnamNet/Hanoi Times, "Vietnam kicks off online residence registration," 2 Jul 2021, https://vietnamnet.vn/en/vietnam-kicks-off-online-residence-registration-752318.html | Corroborating timeline: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%E1%BB%99_kh%E1%BA%A9u, accessed Jun 2026

disaster_idp.characterization: Disaster and climate adaptation capacity is strong, including planned relocation in the Mekong Delta, but relocation is framed as spatial development rather than displacement protection.

public_systems.characterization: Health, education, poverty targeting, and social insurance are administered through territorially anchored systems. Moving across commune or registration boundaries can disrupt access regardless of underlying vulnerability.
public_systems.pattern: Service access tied to registered residence

institutional.characterization: Whole-of-government coherence is strong where it serves state spatial and development objectives. The issue is not weak coordination but the orientation of coordination toward territorial control rather than longitudinal protection.

fiscal.characterization: Fiscal responsibility is tied to local administrative units and state development plans. Resettlement support is often project or programme based rather than embedded as portable long-term entitlement.

data.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): the underlying data architecture for residence-based access shifted in 2023 from paper household registration books to a centralized, ID-number-linked digital population database. This could plausibly be read as a portability improvement (digital records travel more easily than paper books) or as a more efficient version of the same territorial-control logic (the database is still organized around registered residence, and de-registration/re-registration on relocation still appears to govern access). The characterization should specify which reading the evidence supports rather than describing the pre-2023 paper mechanism as if it were still current.
mechanisms: National population database with personal identification code (post-2023)
Residence registration tied to local Cong An (police) administration
Mekong resettlement and household relocation records (unchanged)
data.sources: Vietnam Law on Residence 2020 (effective 1 Jul 2021); household registration books ceased validity 1 Jan 2023, replaced by ID-number-linked national population database. VietnamNet/Hanoi Times, "Vietnam kicks off online residence registration," 2 Jul 2021, https://vietnamnet.vn/en/vietnam-kicks-off-online-residence-registration-752318.html | Corroborating timeline: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%E1%BB%99_kh%E1%BA%A9u, accessed Jun 2026

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure and planned relocation are central. Resettlement compensation exists, but records and support do not consistently integrate with poverty or service systems after households cross administrative boundaries.

countries.name: Cambodia
countries.sovereignty: Territorial state-directed sovereignty with weaker capacity
countries.role: Lower Sesan 2, land concessions, commune-boundary resettlement disruption.
countries.summary: Cambodia illustrates how infrastructure and land-based displacement can be managed through project or commune systems without sustained transfer into public services and social protection. Since 2020, the IDPoor social registry has shifted from a fixed three-year survey cycle to on-demand digital registration, formalized by the 2023 CRVSID law and a 2025 biometric eID rollout -- narrowing the registry-cycle gap for IDPoor specifically, though cross-system interoperability with project-level and commune-bounded mechanisms remains the more central constraint this case identifies.

mobility.characterization: The primary mobility issue is labour outmigration to Thailand, while internal displacement is driven largely by land concessions, infrastructure, and dam construction. Refugee law exists but is weakly implemented.

disaster_idp.characterization: Flood and drought governance exists through NCDM, but internal displacement is not recognized through a dedicated longitudinal framework. Climate and Mekong hydrology pressures increase displacement risk.

public_systems.characterization: IDPoor and Health Equity Funds provide important targeting and health-financing mechanisms, but commune-based administration and three-year update cycles create portability and timing gaps for displaced households.

institutional.characterization: Coordination is often driven by development priorities, party-state control, and project management units rather than a protection-oriented displacement framework.

fiscal.characterization: Displacement-related support is commonly handled through project compensation, donor-supported programmes, or locally administered services rather than portable fiscal responsibility.

data.characterization: Update (Jun 2026): IDPoor, Cambodia's national poverty/social-protection registry, has moved from a fixed three-year survey cycle to on-demand digital registration -- piloted in 2017, scaled nationally from 2020 under COVID-19 pressure, and now formalized by the 2023 Law on Civil Registration, Vital Statistics, and Identification (CRVSID), with a biometric, QR-verified eID rolling out from 2025. The previous registry-cycle gap (households missing a survey round had to wait years for the next) has been substantially narrowed for IDPoor specifically, though the broader question of cross-system interoperability (IDPoor vs. civil registration vs. other sectoral databases) and whether mobile or displaced households can register on demand at their actual location remains less clear from public documentation. The existing characterization should be checked against whether it is describing this current on-demand architecture or the earlier fixed-cycle system.
mechanisms: 2023 Law on Civil Registration, Vital Statistics, and Identification (CRVSID)
data.sources: Biometric Update, "CRVS law reshapes governance and social protection in Cambodia," 27 Mar 2025, https://www.biometricupdate.com/202503/crvs-law-reshapes-governance-and-social-protection-in-cambodia | Biometric Update, "Cambodia introduces new eID card with biometrics and QR codes," 24 Apr 2025, https://www.biometricupdate.com/202504/cambodia-introduces-new-eid-card-with-biometrics-and-qr-codes | Healthy DEvelopments (BMZ), "IDPoor: The cornerstone of Cambodia's social protection system," 5 Jul 2024, https://health.bmz.de/studies/idpoor-the-cornerstone-of-cambodias-social-protection-system/ (on-demand registration piloted 2017, scaled nationally from 2020)

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure displacement is central. Lower Sesan 2 shows how compensation and relocation packages can be provided while service access and vulnerability continue beyond the project boundary.

countries.sources: Biometric Update, "CRVS law reshapes governance and social protection in Cambodia," 27 Mar 2025, https://www.biometricupdate.com/202503/crvs-law-reshapes-governance-and-social-protection-in-cambodia | Biometric Update, "Cambodia introduces new eID card with biometrics and QR codes," 24 Apr 2025, https://www.biometricupdate.com/202504/cambodia-introduces-new-eid-card-with-biometrics-and-qr-codes

countries.name: Lao PDR
countries.role: Xayaburi, Nam Ou/Nam Theun dam displacement, highland resettlement, safeguard gap.
countries.summary: Lao PDR is the clearest infrastructure-finance case. Project safeguards and resettlement action plans can manage bounded impacts but do not govern downstream, cross-jurisdictional, or long-term livelihood disruption.

mobility.characterization: Mobility governance is dominated by labour emigration to Thailand and state-directed resettlement of highland communities. Inward refugee governance is limited despite formal Convention accession.

disaster_idp.characterization: Disaster governance exists but remains weaker than in larger neighbours. The Attapeu dam collapse exposed the interface between disaster response, infrastructure liability, and displaced-community support.

public_systems.characterization: National social protection infrastructure is limited, especially in rural and resettled areas. Health, education, and welfare access often depend on village location and project-specific support.

institutional.characterization: Whole-of-government coherence is organized around development and infrastructure priorities. Resettlement is handled through project bodies and line ministries rather than a cross-sectoral IDP protection mandate.

fiscal.characterization: Displacement support is usually project financed and bounded by resettlement action plans. Limited ordinary social protection capacity makes fiscal embedding difficult after project closure.

data.characterization: Administrative and social protection data systems are less developed than in Indonesia, Vietnam, or the Philippines. Project records rarely provide a pathway into broader social support.

infra_finance.characterization: Hydropower and corridor infrastructure are central. Xayaburi illustrates the mismatch between safeguard-calibrated project obligations and displacement as a longitudinal, cross-border livelihood condition.

cases.name: Health Insurance Card Scheme (HICS)
cases.function: Strongest positive absorption case. Shows how public-health and cost-recovery rationales can extend coverage without formal protection status.

cases.name: Pink cards / stateless persons
cases.function: Shows how administrative documentation can provide access while restricting mobility and resetting eligibility across provincial boundaries.

cases.name: Rohingya Bangladesh-to-Malaysia
cases.function: UNHCR registration in Bangladesh does not travel to Malaysia; protection needs continue while status and recognition reset.

cases.name: Palu earthquake (2018)
cases.function: Disaster-affected households were reclassified as internal migrants or excluded after emergency and registry cycles shifted.

cases.name: Presidential Regulation 125/2016
cases.function: Positive whole-of-government design assigning cross-ministerial responsibilities, while implementation remains fiscally decentralised.

cases.name: Marawi siege (2017)
cases.function: Shows how strong emergency coordination can fail to produce a single longitudinal mandate for protracted displacement.

cases.name: 4Ps / Listahanan after Haiyan
cases.pattern: Partial fiscal absorption with portability limits
cases.function: Shows the promise and limits of registry-linked social protection when households move across municipal boundaries.

cases.name: Myanmar social protection collapse
cases.function: Outer diagnostic boundary: national systems were not merely weak but reversed, requiring reconstruction before absorption can occur.

cases.name: Vietnam hộ khẩu system
cases.function: Shows that data fragmentation can be a design feature of territorial control rather than a technical deficit.

cases.name: Lower Sesan 2 Dam
cases.function: Compensation and relocation did not ensure portable service access after commune-boundary disruption.

cases.name: Xayaburi Dam
cases.function: Shows why safeguards calibrated to project closure cannot govern downstream and long-term livelihood disruption.

cases.name: 2025-26 refugee work-permit and biometric ID formalisation
cases.pattern: Sovereignty-preserving functional access, with phased formalisation for a defined cohort
cases.function: The Aug 2025 cabinet resolution granted work permits to long-staying Myanmar refugees in the nine border camps (population approx. 80,000-81,000, of whom roughly 42,000 of working age); more than 5,500 had joined the workforce by mid-2026. In Jun 2026 the Royal Thai Government began issuing biometric, QR-verified, non-Thai ID cards recognising recipients as 'Myanmar Displaced Persons' and integrating them into the national civil registry, enabling banking, telecommunications, and healthcare access. Issuance is phased by age (5+) and prioritises those already employed. This is the most significant formalisation of camp-based refugee status since the camps were established in the 1980s, and the first instance of civil-registry integration for this population -- but it is bounded (non-citizen, employment- and age-phased) and distinct from asylum recognition. Non-refoulement practice for other populations (e.g., the Feb 2025 deportation of detained Uyghur asylum seekers) remains inconsistent, underscoring that formalisation is selective rather than systemic.
cases.sources: UNHCR Thailand, "Government-issued ID cards mark new era of refugee inclusion and self-reliance in Thailand," press release, 17 Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/th/en/government-issued-id-cards-mark-new-era-of-refugee-inclusion%20and%20self-reliance-in-thailand | UNHCR, "UNHCR welcomes Royal Thai Government resolution providing long-term refugees the right to work," press release, 26 Aug 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/news/press-releases/unhcr-welcomes-royal-thai-government-resolution-providing-long-term-refugees | Human Rights Watch, "Thailand Allows Myanmar Refugees in Camps to Work Legally," 27 Aug 2025, https://www.hrw.org/news/2025/08/27/thailand-allows-myanmar-refugees-in-camps-to-work-legally | The Diplomat, "Thai Government Grants Refugees From Myanmar the Right to Work," 27 Aug 2025, https://thediplomat.com/2025/08/thai-government-grants-refugees-from-myanmar-the-right-to-work/

cases.name: Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian (DPP) state-led refugee registration
cases.function: Beginning Jan 2026, Malaysia rolled out the DPP, a biometric registration system built by MIMOS Berhad (the national applied R&D agency) under Home Affairs Ministry control, explicitly intended to replace UNHCR's role in registering and documenting refugees. The Home Minister told parliament DPP would become the sole recognized refugee documentation, with UNHCR's role narrowed to resettlement referrals. More than 210,000 refugees and asylum seekers were UNHCR-registered as of late 2025 (about 90% from Myanmar, including 120,000+ Rohingya); first-phase rollout targeted Rohingya detainees in immigration detention. DPP cards do not confer legal status, a right to work generally, or protection from arrest; officials describe them as bridging documentation pending resettlement, even as resettlement departures fell sharply (1,970 in 2025 vs 8,627 in 2024) following US resettlement cuts. No published data-protection safeguards or appeal mechanism have been identified, and mass immigration enforcement (approx. 92,000 arrests in 2025) continued throughout the rollout, including arrests of people holding UNHCR documentation. This is a categorical-fault-line case in which the state inserts itself as registering authority while the underlying exclusion from legal status is preserved -- a distinct mechanism from simple non-recognition, worth tracking as it may prefigure similar moves elsewhere in the region.
cases.sources: Human Rights Watch, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration System Raises Concerns," 4 May 2026, https://www.hrw.org/news/2026/05/04/malaysia-new-refugee-registration-system-raises-concerns | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: Rohingya Refugees Held Indefinitely in Inhumane and Degrading Conditions," 12 Mar 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-03-12/ | Fortify Rights, "Malaysia: New Refugee Registration Scheme Must Protect Rights," 1 Jun 2026, https://www.fortifyrights.org/mly-inv-2026-06-01/ | UNHCR Malaysia, "The UN Refugee Agency," accessed Jun 2026, https://www.unhcr.org/my/ (confirms DPP launched Jan 2026 as government-led registration system) | New Straits Times, "Refugee policy proposal to be presented to cabinet by mid-June," 19 May 2026, https://www.nst.com.my/amp/news/nation/2026/05/1443346/refugee-policy-proposal-be-presented-cabinet-mid-june

cases.name: March 2025 earthquake and compounding humanitarian financing contraction
cases.pattern: Compounding collapse: disaster displacement layered on institutional reversal amid financing contraction
cases.function: The Mar 2025 magnitude-7.7 earthquake struck central Myanmar, damaging tens of thousands of houses, dozens of roads and bridges, and nearly 70 health facilities, and disrupting agriculture for an estimated 2 million newly affected people. It compounded conflict-driven displacement that had already reached 3.6 million by late 2025; OCHA's 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan projects this rising to approximately 4 million during 2026. Reflecting funding contraction rather than reduced need, the 2026 plan narrowed its scope to life-saving and protection activities in conflict- and earthquake-affected townships (about two-thirds of the country), down from a nationwide caseload, after only 26% of the 2025 appeal was funded. This case illustrates a distinct failure mode from the 2021 institutional-reversal case already in the database: here, disaster and conflict displacement compound each other at the same time the international financing that has substituted for collapsed domestic fiscal capacity is itself receding -- a scenario the chapter's fiscal-architecture argument should anticipate as the limit case within the limit case.
cases.sources: OCHA, "Myanmar Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan 2026," 10 Dec 2025, https://www.unocha.org/publications/report/myanmar/myanmar-humanitarian-needs-and-response-plan-2026 | World Food Programme, "WFP warns that Myanmar faces rising displacement and unacceptable hunger levels in 2026," https://www.wfp.org/news/wfp-warns-myanmar-faces-rising-displacement-and-unacceptable-hunger-levels-2026
